reasoning: There is no evidence that South Korea, Japan, Poland, or any other US ally has acquired a nuclear weapon as of November 2025, nor is there any credible indication that they will successfully do so by the end of the year. The process of acquiring a nuclear weapon involves years of infrastructure development, enrichment or reprocessing, testing, and immense geopolitical ramifications, including withdrawing from the Non-Proliferation Treaty. While recent media reports and op-eds reflect ongoing debate about whether these countries should pursue nuclear weapons in response to regional threats or shifts in US policy, none indicate that actual weaponization has occurred or is imminent. Given the negligible base rate of sudden, secret nuclear proliferation and the strict resolution criteria requiring official confirmation from credible agencies or governments, the probability of a US ally genuinely possessing a nuclear weapon by December 31, 2025 constitutes a virtual impossibility.
